title: The Challenging Job Market for Recent College Graduates
content:
What a challenging moment it is to be graduating from college and trying to start a professional career. Artificial intelligence is in the midst of radically transforming, and possibly destroying, entry-level white-collar work. Although the US economy is technically still growing, major employers are laying off workers in droves. Last year, the government slashed hundreds of thousands of academic and nonprofit jobs. At the end of 2025, the number of job openings dropped to the lowest level since September 2020.

An analysis from the Federal Reserve Bank of New York shows that the unemployment rate for recent college graduates has grown higher than the national average for all workers. Entering the workforce is especially tough right now, and a recent survey predicted that 2026 would be the worst job market for new grads since the pandemic began. All of this is happening against the backdrop of a long-term trend that challenges the conventional wisdom about the value of a college degree. There simply aren't enough degree-requiring jobs to sustain the number of new graduates entering the market. By the end of last year, underemployment of recent college grads reached almost 43 percent.

It is no wonder that the millions of students graduating this year feel tremendous pressure. They must navigate AI application screening systems and connect with real hiring managers just to find a company that is actually hiring entry-level workers. The process feels like an obstacle course where one must carefully balance without falling into the water below. While finding a job is not impossible, the topsy-turvy economy requires aggressive pursuit of multiple avenues. Graduates must maintain an openness to pursue career paths entirely different from what they had initially planned.
